summary: McKenzie describes a creative SEO-based anti-piracy tactic for his Bingo Card Creator software. He proposes 'Google bombing' piracy-related search terms by linking them to a funny YouTube video instead of actual cracks. He encourages other shareware authors to do the same for their software, creating a collective defense against software pirates. He also notes that crackers eventually updated their keygen for his new version, but treats it as a minor nuisance he can quickly counter.
key_points: Software piracy can be combated through SEO tactics rather than just technical protection — flooding search results for crack-related terms with decoy links
Google bombs work because piracy sites rarely get trusted inbound links, making it easy for legitimate blogs to outrank them
Download sites can help by including serial/keygen keywords naturally on their pages, further burying actual piracy results
Technical protection like breaking keygens is a cat-and-mouse game, but SEO manipulation provides a more durable defense
money_quotes: If you are a shareware author, an uISV, or if you just want to take a stand against software piracy, you can do so in thirty seconds.
Blam, instant Google bomb — or should we say, Google Cannon.

Just a few people linking a small program will get that as the #1 result, and a few more people will cause it to rank highly for any query including words like keygen, serial, etc, because our poor pirate friends don't typically get trusted links from anybody.

It seems like that cracker group finally got around to realizing that their old keygen didn't work anymore (the comments I saw were priceless — unrepeatable, but priceless), so they went ahead and cracked version 1.04.
I think every download site should implement similar language, because doing so would make it impossible to find the needle in the haystack.
key_insight: SEO can be weaponized as an anti-piracy tool by flooding crack-related search terms with legitimate links, exploiting the fact that piracy sites have weak link authority.
